Semiconductor Electron Theory: From Band Structure to p-n Junctions ​

📅 Date: 2026-04-02 | 🏷️ Tags: Solid State Physics, Semiconductors, p-n Junctions | 📂 Category: Condensed Matter Notes

Introduction ​

Semiconductors occupy a unique position in condensed matter physics, bridging the gap between insulating and metallic behavior. Unlike metals where the Fermi level lies within a partially filled band, semiconductors have a small band gap separating a filled valence band from an empty conduction band.

This article provides a comprehensive treatment of semiconductor physics, with detailed derivations of all statistical mechanics formulas and explicit explanations of solid-state physics concepts.

Part I: Band Structure and Impurity Levels ​

1.1 The Fundamental Difference: Metals vs. Semiconductors ​

Energy Band Diagram

In the single-electron approximation, electrons in a crystal occupy energy bands. The key distinction between materials arises from the position of the Fermi energy EF relative to these bands:

  • Metals: The Fermi level lies within a partially filled band, allowing electrons to move freely and conduct electricity even at T=0.

  • Insulators: The Fermi level lies in a large band gap (Eg>5 eV). At room temperature, kBT≈0.025 eV, so thermal excitation across the gap is negligible: e−Eg/kBT∼e−200≈0.

  • Semiconductors: The Fermi level lies in a small gap (Eg∼0.1-3 eV). For Eg=1 eV:

EgkBT=1 eV0.025 eV≈40

While e−40≈4×10−18 is small, when multiplied by the enormous density of states (∼1019 cm−3), it gives a measurable carrier concentration (ni∼1010 cm−3 for Si).

1.2 Intrinsic Semiconductors: Electron-Hole Pairs ​

Thermal Generation of Carriers

In a pure (intrinsic) semiconductor, thermal energy excites electrons from the valence band to the conduction band. This process creates:

Valence band electron+thermal energy→Conduction band electron+Valence band hole

Each excitation produces an electron-hole pair:

  • Electron: Negative charge (−e), resides in conduction band, effective mass me∗
  • Hole: Positive charge (+e), represents absence of electron in valence band, effective mass mh∗

Charge Neutrality in Intrinsic Semiconductors

Since electrons and holes are created in pairs:

n=p=ni

where ni is the intrinsic carrier concentration.

1.3 Extrinsic Semiconductors: Doping and Impurity Levels ​

Donors and Acceptors

Impurities are intentionally introduced to control carrier concentration:

  • Donors (e.g., P, As in Si): Have one more valence electron than the host. This extra electron is weakly bound and easily ionized: ND→ND++e−.

  • Acceptors (e.g., B, Al in Si): Have one fewer valence electron. They capture electrons from the valence band, creating holes: NA+e−→NA− (or equivalently, NA→NA−+h+).

The Hydrogenic Model for Impurities

Consider a donor atom with effective charge +e (nucleus + core electrons) and one loosely bound electron. In the semiconductor medium:

  1. Dielectric screening: The Coulomb potential is reduced by the dielectric constant ε:

    V(r)=−e24πε0εr

  2. Effective mass: The electron moves with effective mass m∗ rather than free electron mass m0.

Detailed Derivation of Binding Energy

The Schrödinger equation for the donor electron:

(−ℏ22me∗∇2−e24πε0εr)ψ(r)=Eψ(r)

Compare to the hydrogen atom equation:

(−ℏ22m0∇2−e24πε0r)ψ(r)=Eψ(r)

The hydrogen ground state energy is:

EH=−m0e42(4πε0)2ℏ2=−13.6 eV

By dimensional analysis, replacing m0→me∗ and e2→e2/ε:

ED=−me∗m0⋅1ε2⋅13.6 eV

Numerical Estimate for Silicon

For Si: ε≈11.7, me∗/m0≈0.26 (conductivity effective mass)

ED=−0.26×1(11.7)2×13.6 eV≈−0.026 eV=−26 meV

This is indeed much smaller than the Si band gap (Eg=1.12 eV), confirming that donor levels lie just below the conduction band edge.

Effective Bohr Radius

The Bohr radius in hydrogen is a0=4πε0ℏ2/(m0e2)≈0.53 Å. The effective Bohr radius for donor states:

aB∗=εm0me∗a0=11.70.26×0.53 Å≈24 Å=2.4 nm

Since aB∗≫alattice≈0.5 nm, the electron orbit encompasses many unit cells, justifying the use of effective mass approximation and continuum dielectric theory.

Part II: Statistical Mechanics of Carriers ​

2.1 Why Maxwell-Boltzmann Statistics Apply ​

The Fermi-Dirac Distribution

In equilibrium, the probability of occupying a state with energy E is:

f(E)=1e(E−EF)/kBT+1

The Non-Degeneracy Condition

In semiconductors, the Fermi level lies in the band gap, far from both band edges:

EC−EF≫kBTandEF−EV≫kBT

For the conduction band, since E≥EC:

E−EF≥EC−EF≫kBT

Therefore:

e(E−EF)/kBT≫1

And the Fermi-Dirac distribution simplifies to:

f(E)≈1e(E−EF)/kBT=e−(E−EF)/kBT=e−(EC−EF)/kBT⋅e−(E−EC)/kBT

This is the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution. The key difference from metals is:

AspectMetalsSemiconductors
Fermi level positionInside bandIn gap
Occupation at EFf(EF)=1/2f(EC)≪1
StatisticsFermi-Dirac (degenerate)Maxwell-Boltzmann (non-degenerate)
Pauli exclusionDominantNegligible

Physical Interpretation

Because the conduction band is nearly empty (f(E)≪1), the probability of two electrons occupying the same state is negligible. Electrons behave as distinguishable classical particles, enabling the use of Maxwell-Boltzmann statistics and greatly simplifying calculations.

2.2 Derivation of Electron and Hole Concentrations ​

Density of States in the Conduction Band

Near the conduction band minimum, electrons behave as free particles with effective mass me∗:

E(k)=EC+ℏ2k22me∗

To find the density of states gC(E), we count the number of k-states per unit energy interval. In 3D, the number of states with wavevector magnitude less than k is:

N(k)=2×43πk3(2π)3/V=Vk33π2

The factor of 2 accounts for spin degeneracy. Converting to energy using E=EC+ℏ2k2/(2me∗):

k=2me∗(E−EC)ℏ2

Taking the derivative:

gC(E)=1VdNdE=1VdNdkdkdE=k2π2⋅me∗ℏ2k=(2me∗)3/22π2ℏ3E−EC

for E≥EC, and zero otherwise.

Calculating the Electron Concentration

The total electron concentration is:

n=∫EC∞gC(E)f(E)dE

Substituting our expressions:

n=(2me∗)3/22π2ℏ3∫EC∞E−EC⋅e−(E−EF)/kBTdE

Step-by-Step Integration

Let x=E−EC, so E−EF=x+(EC−EF):

n=(2me∗)3/22π2ℏ3e−(EC−EF)/kBT∫0∞x1/2e−x/kBTdx

The integral is a Gamma function. Let u=x/kBT, so dx=kBTdu:

∫0∞x1/2e−x/kBTdx=(kBT)3/2∫0∞u1/2e−udu=(kBT)3/2Γ(32)

Since Γ(3/2)=12Γ(1/2)=π2:

n=(2me∗)3/22π2ℏ3e−(EC−EF)/kBT⋅(kBT)3/2⋅π2

Simplifying:

n=2(me∗kBT2πℏ2)3/2e−(EC−EF)/kBT

Effective Density of States

We define the effective density of states in the conduction band:

NC=2(me∗kBT2πℏ2)3/2

This allows us to write the electron concentration compactly as:

n=NCe−(EC−EF)/kBT

Physical Interpretation: The electron concentration equals the effective number of states at the conduction band edge (NC), multiplied by the Boltzmann factor for the energy difference between the band edge and the Fermi level.

Hole Concentration

By similar analysis for holes in the valence band (with effective mass mh∗):

p=NVe−(EF−EV)/kBT

where:

NV=2(mh∗kBT2πℏ2)3/2

Note that the exponent is (EF−EV) rather than (EV−EF) because holes have positive energy when they are lower in the band diagram (closer to the valence band maximum).

Numerical Values at Room Temperature

For silicon (me∗≈1.08m0, mh∗≈0.56m0):

NC=2(1.08×9.11×10−31×1.38×10−23×3002π×(1.05×10−34)2)3/2≈2.8×1019 cm−3NV≈1.0×1019 cm−3

2.3 The Law of Mass Action ​

Derivation

Multiply the expressions for electron and hole concentrations:

np=NCNVe−(EC−EF)/kBT⋅e−(EF−EV)/kBT=NCNVe−(EC−EV)/kBT

The Fermi level cancels out! Defining the band gap Eg=EC−EV:

np=NCNVe−Eg/kBT

This is the law of mass action for semiconductors. At a given temperature, the product of electron and hole concentrations is constant, independent of doping or Fermi level position.

Intrinsic Carrier Concentration

For an intrinsic (pure) semiconductor, charge neutrality requires n=p=ni. From the mass action law:

ni2=NCNVe−Eg/kBT

Therefore:

ni=NCNVe−Eg/2kBT

This shows that ni depends exponentially on Eg/(2kBT). The factor of 2 in the exponent is crucial—it means that creating an electron-hole pair requires thermal energy of order Eg/2 per carrier, not Eg.

Numerical Example for Silicon

At T=300 K with Eg=1.12 eV:

ni=2.8×1019×1.0×1019⋅e−1.12/(2×0.025)≈1.5×1010 cm−3

This is much smaller than the atomic density of Si (5×1022 cm−3), confirming that only a tiny fraction of atoms contribute carriers.

Position of Intrinsic Fermi Level

Setting n=p for intrinsic material:

NCe−(EC−Ei)/kBT=NVe−(Ei−EV)/kBT

Taking the natural logarithm:

ln⁡NC−EC−EikBT=ln⁡NV−Ei−EVkBT

Solving for Ei:

2Ei=EC+EV+kBTln⁡(NCNV)Ei=EC+EV2+34kBTln⁡(mh∗me∗)

If mh∗=me∗, the intrinsic Fermi level lies exactly at mid-gap. For Si with mh∗/me∗≈0.52, the correction is small (≈kBT).

2.4 Doped Semiconductors: Charge Neutrality and Fermi Level ​

Donor Ionization Statistics

Consider an n-type semiconductor with donor concentration ND. The fraction of ionized donors is given by:

ND+ND=1−f(ED)=11+e−(ED−EF)/kBT

At room temperature, since donor binding energies are small (EC−ED∼26 meV ∼kBT), most donors are ionized:

ND+≈ND

Charge Neutrality Equation

The total positive charge equals the total negative charge:

p+ND+=n+NA−

For an n-type semiconductor with negligible acceptors:

n=p+ND+

At moderate temperatures where ni≪ND, we have p=ni2/n≪n≈ND. Therefore:

n≈ND

Finding the Fermi Level

Using n=NCe−(EC−EF)/kBT=ND:

EC−EF=kBTln⁡(NCND)EF=EC−kBTln⁡(NCND)

Numerical Example

For Si with NC=2.8×1019 cm−3 and ND=1016 cm−3:

EC−EF=0.025 eV×ln⁡(2800)≈0.025×7.9≈0.20 eV

The Fermi level lies 0.20 eV below the conduction band, well within the gap but much closer to EC than to mid-gap (Eg/2=0.56 eV).

Part III: Direct and Indirect Band Gaps ​

3.1 Momentum Conservation in Optical Transitions ​

Photon Momentum vs. Electron Momentum

When a photon is absorbed, exciting an electron from the valence band to the conduction band, both energy and momentum must be conserved.

For a photon with energy Ephoton=hν=Eg:

pphoton=hνc=Egc

For Eg=1 eV:

pphoton=1.6×10−19 J3×108 m/s≈5.3×10−28 kg m/s

Compare to the typical electron momentum at the Brillouin zone boundary:

pBZ∼ℏa∼1.05×10−340.5×10−9≈2×10−25 kg m/s

The ratio is:

pphotonpBZ∼5×10−282×10−25∼10−3

Conclusion: The photon momentum is negligible compared to the Brillouin zone scale. Therefore, optical transitions are essentially vertical in k-space—the electron wavevector does not change during the transition.

3.2 Direct Band Gap Semiconductors ​

Band Structure

In a direct gap semiconductor (e.g., GaAs, InP), the conduction band minimum and valence band maximum occur at the same k-point (typically k=0, the Γ point).

Absorption Coefficient

Since momentum conservation is automatically satisfied, the absorption coefficient for direct transitions is:

α(hν)∝hν−Eg

for hν>Eg. Above the band gap, absorption is strong (α∼104 cm−1), meaning light is absorbed within about 1 μm of the surface.

3.3 Indirect Band Gap Semiconductors ​

Band Structure of Silicon

In Si (indirect gap):

  • Valence band maximum: at k=0 (Γ point)
  • Conduction band minimum: at k≈0.85×(2π/a) along the [100] direction (near the X point)

The minimum energy gap is Eg=1.12 eV, but a vertical transition from the valence band maximum to the conduction band minimum would violate momentum conservation by Δk≈0.85×(2π/a).

Phonon-Assisted Transitions

To conserve momentum, the transition requires assistance from a phonon (quantized lattice vibration). The process is:

  1. Photon is absorbed (provides energy, negligible momentum)
  2. Phonon is absorbed or emitted (provides momentum ℏq≈ℏΔk)

Transition Rate

Using second-order perturbation theory, the transition rate involves:

  • Photon-electron matrix element
  • Phonon-electron matrix element
  • Energy denominator from intermediate virtual state

The absorption coefficient is:

αindirect(hν)∝(hν−Eg±ℏωphonon)2

The ± corresponds to phonon absorption or emission. The quadratic dependence (vs. square root for direct gaps) reflects the phase space available for two-particle processes.

Comparison

PropertyDirect Gap (GaAs)Indirect Gap (Si)
Absorption coefficient at hν=Eg+0.1 eV∼104 cm−1∼102 cm−1
Radiative recombination rateFast (∼ ns)Slow (∼ ms)
ApplicationLEDs, lasersElectronics, solar cells

Part IV: Transport Properties ​

4.1 Drift Current and Mobility ​

Equation of Motion

Under an electric field E, electrons experience force F=−eE. Between collisions, they accelerate. With collision time τn, the average drift velocity is:

vd,n=−eτnme∗E=−μnE

where the electron mobility is defined as:

μn=eτnme∗

Similarly for holes:

μp=eτpmh∗

Conductivity

The total current density is the sum of electron and hole contributions:

J=(−en)vd,n+(ep)vd,p=e(nμn+pμp)E

The conductivity is:

σ=e(nμn+pμp)

Resistivity

ρ=1σ=1e(nμn+pμp)

4.2 Temperature Dependence of Mobility ​

Scattering Mechanisms

Three main processes limit mobility:

  1. Acoustic Phonon Scattering (dominant at moderate temperatures):

    • Scattering rate: 1/τ∝T
    • Mobility: μ∝T−3/2

  2. Ionized Impurity Scattering (dominant at low temperatures):

    • Coulomb scattering from charged donors/acceptors
    • Brooks-Herring formula: μ∝T3/2/Nimp

  3. Optical Phonon Scattering (dominant at high temperatures):

    • Requires ℏωoptical∼kBT
    • Strong temperature dependence: μ∝eℏω/kBT

Matthiessen's Rule

When multiple scattering mechanisms are present:

1μ=1μphonon+1μimpurity+…

The mechanism with the shortest mean free time (highest scattering rate) dominates.

4.3 The Hall Effect ​

Experimental Geometry

Current Jx flows in x-direction, magnetic field Bz is applied in z-direction. A transverse electric field Ey (Hall field) develops to balance the Lorentz force.

Steady-State Condition

For electrons with velocity vx:

Fy=−e(Ey−vxBz)=0

Therefore:

Ey=vxBz=−JxneBz

Hall Coefficient

Defining RH=Ey/(JxBz):

RH=−1ne

For holes (positive charge):

RH=+1pe

Significance

The sign of RH directly identifies the carrier type:

  • RH<0: n-type (electron conduction)
  • RH>0: p-type (hole conduction)

The magnitude gives the carrier concentration.

Part V: The p-n Junction ​

5.1 Formation and Equilibrium ​

Diffusion and Drift

When p-type and n-type semiconductors are brought together:

  1. Electrons diffuse from n-side (high n) to p-side (low n)
  2. Holes diffuse from p-side (high p) to n-side (low p)
  3. This leaves behind:
    • Positive ionized donors (ND+) on n-side
    • Negative ionized acceptors (NA−) on p-side
  4. The resulting electric field opposes further diffusion
  5. At equilibrium: drift current = diffusion current for each carrier type

5.2 Built-in Potential ​

Fermi Level Alignment

At equilibrium, the Fermi level must be constant throughout the device. In the bulk regions:

EF,n=EC−kBTln⁡(NCND)EF,p=EV+kBTln⁡(NVNA)

The built-in potential energy is the difference:

eVbi=EF,n−EF,p=Eg−kBTln⁡(NCND)−kBTln⁡(NVNA)

Using ni2=NCNVe−Eg/kBT:

eVbi=kBTln⁡(NDNAni2)

Numerical Example

For Si with ND=NA=1016 cm−3 and ni=1.5×1010 cm−3:

Vbi=0.025 eV×ln⁡(10322.25×1020)=0.025×ln⁡(4.4×1011)≈0.69 V

5.3 Depletion Region Analysis ​

Charge Density

Assume abrupt junction at x=0. In the depletion region:

  • n-side (0<x<xn): ρ=+eND
  • p-side (−xp<x<0): ρ=−eNA

Poisson's Equation

d2ϕdx2=−ρε

On the n-side:

d2ϕdx2=−eNDε

Integrating with boundary condition dϕ/dx=0 at x=xn:

dϕdx=eNDε(xn−x)

Integrating again:

ϕ(x)=ϕn−eND2ε(xn−x)2

Similarly for the p-side with ϕ(−xp)=ϕp:

ϕ(x)=ϕp+eNA2ε(x+xp)2

Continuity Conditions

At x=0:

  1. Potential continuous: ϕ(0−)=ϕ(0+)
  2. Electric field continuous: dϕ/dx|0−=dϕ/dx|0+

From condition 2:

eNDxn=eNAxp

This expresses charge neutrality: total positive charge equals total negative charge.

Depletion Width

Defining total width W=xn+xp:

xn=NAND+NAW,xp=NDND+NAW

The potential difference is:

Vbi=ϕn−ϕp=e2ε(NDxn2+NAxp2)=e2εNDNAND+NAW2

Solving for W:

W=2εVbie(1ND+1NA)

For asymmetric junction with ND≫NA:

W≈2εVbieNA

The depletion region extends primarily into the lightly doped side.

5.4 Current-Voltage Characteristics ​

Shockley Diode Equation

Under applied bias V:

  • Forward bias (V>0): Barrier reduced to e(Vbi−V)
  • Reverse bias (V<0): Barrier increased to e(Vbi+|V|)

The current-voltage relationship is:

J=J0(eeV/kBT−1)

Saturation Current Derivation

The saturation current J0 is the sum of minority carrier diffusion currents:

J0=e(Dnnp0Ln+Dppn0Lp)

where:

  • np0=ni2/NA (equilibrium minority electron concentration on p-side)
  • pn0=ni2/ND (equilibrium minority hole concentration on n-side)
  • Dn, Dp are diffusion coefficients
  • Ln=Dnτn, Lp=Dpτp are diffusion lengths

Using the Einstein relation D=(kBT/e)μ:

J0=ekBT(μnnp0Ln+μppn0Lp)

Forward Bias

For V≫kBT/e:

J≈J0eeV/kBT

The current increases exponentially. A 60 mV increase (at room temperature) multiplies current by e0.06/0.025≈10.

Reverse Bias

For V<0 with |V|≫kBT/e:

J≈−J0

The current saturates at the small value −J0.

5.5 Junction Capacitance ​

Depletion Capacitance

The depletion region acts as a parallel plate capacitor:

Cj=εAW

Substituting the expression for W:

Cj=AeεNAND2(NA+ND)(Vbi−V)

For asymmetric junction (ND≫NA):

Cj≈AeεNA2(Vbi−V)

The capacitance varies as (Vbi−V)−1/2. This voltage dependence is used in varactor diodes for tuning applications.

Summary ​

ParameterSymbolFormula
Electron concentrationnNCexp⁡[−(EC−EF)/kBT]
Hole concentrationpNVexp⁡[−(EF−EV)/kBT]
Intrinsic carrier concentrationniNCNVexp⁡[−Eg/2kBT]
Mass action lawnpni2
Conductivityσe(nμn+pμp)
Built-in potentialVbi(kBT/e)ln⁡(NDNA/ni2)
Depletion widthW2εVbi/e⋅(1/ND+1/NA)
Diode currentJJ0[exp⁡(eV/kBT)−1]

Semiconductors exemplify how quantum mechanics (band theory) and statistical mechanics (carrier distributions) combine to determine macroscopic properties. The transition from degenerate Fermi-Dirac statistics in metals to classical Maxwell-Boltzmann statistics in semiconductors is a key insight that enables the rich variety of phenomena underlying modern electronics.
